item.title: Capitolul 1: Structura argumentului
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Orice argument valid are trei componente: afirmația (ceea ce susții), dovada (ce demonstrează afirmația) și raționamentul (legătura logică dintre ele).

item.title: Chapter 1: The Anatomy of an Argument
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Every argument, however complex, can be reduced to three components: a claim, the evidence that supports it, and the reasoning that connects the two. Understanding this structure is the prerequisite for everything else in this guide.

item.title: Capitolul 2: Sofisme frecvente
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Sofismele sunt erori de raționament care par valide la prima vedere. Recunoașterea lor este esențială pentru evaluarea argumentelor.

item.title: Chapter 2: Evidence and Its Limits
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Evidence does not speak for itself. It is selected, framed, and interpreted. The most important skill in evaluating arguments is not finding more evidence but assessing the quality of the evidence you already have.

item.title: Chapter 3: Fallacies and Misdirection
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: A fallacy is a reasoning error that makes an invalid argument look valid. Learning to recognise them is not about catching people out — it is about protecting your own thinking from patterns that feel compelling but do not actually work.

item.title: Chapter 4: Counter-Arguments and Steelmanning
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The best preparation for making an argument is to understand, as deeply as possible, why a reasonable person might disagree with it. This is not courtesy. It is method.

item.title: Chapter 5: Writing the Argument
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The argument exists in your head before it exists on the page, and the two versions are rarely identical. Writing is not the transcription of finished thinking — it is a stage in the development of the thinking itself.

item.title: Evidence standards in contested historical claims
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Historians regularly work with conflicting sources. The criteria used to decide which source is more credible are methodologically consequential — and almost never made explicit. This piece proposes a framework for stating and defending those criteria openly.

item.title: How archives construct institutional credibility
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Institutional archives preserve the official version of decisions. The informal record — the conversations, the competing pressures, the reasoning that never made it into a memo — is almost always missing. This piece examines what that gap costs, and how to read against it.

item.title: LLMs as research tools: a provisional verdict
item.description: After eight months of deliberate use: useful for specific things, not useful for others, and the line is not where most of the commentary assumes.

item.title: Writing for readers, not for the record
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Academic writing has two audiences: the disciplinary jury that will judge the work, and the actual reader who wants to understand something. These audiences have different needs, and most academic writing is optimised entirely for the first at the expense of the second. The disciplinary jury wants signals of competence: the right citations, the correct theoretical vocabulary, the appropriate hedging. It wants to see that you know the literature and are not making naive mistakes.

item.title: On reading annotated books
item.description: There is something unusually intimate about finding a book whose previous owner has marked it more heavily than you would.

item.title: On writing with sources visible
item.description: There is a style of academic writing that hides its scaffolding — citations appear, conclusions are stated, but the reasoning that connects them is offstage. The reader is asked to trust a process they cannot inspect. I have been trying to write the opposite way. If I am relying on a source for a specific claim, I want the reliance to be visible: not just a footnote number but a sentence that shows how the source earns its place in the argument.

item.title: Electoral systems dataset: first pass complete
item.description: Finished coding the 1990–2024 dataset. One thing that keeps jumping out: countries that adopted mixed-member systems early have significantly lower electoral volatility scores than those that went proportional from the start. Need to check whether this survives controlling for party system age — the two variables are correlated enough that I am not confident the effect is real rather than a proxy. Running the robustness checks this week. The dataset will be published openly when the paper is submitted.

item.title: Tag Taxonomy Standardised
item.description: All articles and announcements have been re-tagged using a standardised taxonomy. Old freeform tags have been mapped to the new controlled vocabulary.

item.title: Three things I changed my mind about this year
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: 1. The value of bad first drafts. I used to try to write reasonably finished prose on the first pass. This turns out to be mostly inhibiting — the energy that should go into finding the argument goes into making the sentences acceptable. I now write the first draft badly on purpose. The resulting text is unreadable, but it is legible to me, and rewriting is much easier than writing.

item.title: Three things I changed my mind about this year
item.description: A mid-year inventory. Changing your mind about things is underrated as a productivity practice.

item.title: Critical Thinking as Infrastructure
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The capacity to evaluate arguments, detect fallacies, and construct coherent reasoning does not emerge spontaneously. It is a skill that must be taught, practised, and assessed — as systematically as mathematics or grammar.

item.title: Gândirea critică în educație
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Capacitatea de a evalua argumente, de a detecta sofisme și de a construi raționamente coerente nu apare spontan. Este o abilitate care se predă, se exersează și se evaluează — la fel ca matematica sau literatura.

item.title: Open Call for Contributors — Summer 2026
item.description: Academis Demo is open to new contributors. We are looking for writers with strong analytical voices in history, technology, urban policy, and media criticism. Work is published under CC BY 4.0.

item.title: Conferences are mostly a waste of time
item.description: Not all conferences. But the value is concentrated in a few hours of the programme, and you generally cannot predict in advance which hours.

item.title: Arendt on the banality of bureaucratic rule
item.description: The phrase “rule by Nobody” does a lot of work here that is easy to miss. Arendt is not describing incompetence or absence of authority — she is describing a structure in which responsibility is so diffused that accountability becomes impossible to locate. The bureaucracy functions; it simply has no face that can be held responsible for what it does. This is the condition she thinks produces the particular kind of moral failure she documented in the Eichmann trial: not monsters, but administrators.

item.title: The credibility deficit
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: There is a pattern I keep encountering in the institutional histories I study: credibility is lost slowly at first, through a sequence of small decisions that each seem defensible in isolation, then rapidly when a single event makes the accumulated cost visible. The slow phase is hard to see from inside the institution. Each compromise is made against a real competing pressure. The incentive to be seen as pragmatic, to preserve working relationships, to avoid costly public confrontations — these are not irrational.

item.title: Contributor Guide Updated — Version 2
item.description: The contributor guide has been substantially revised to clarify pitch format requirements, our review timeline, and what editors look for in a strong argument pitch.

item.title: Podcast fatigue is a format problem
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Podcast fatigue is not about having too many podcasts to listen to. It is about the hour-long conversation format having colonised categories of content where it adds nothing and obscures quite a lot. An edited monologue delivered in writing takes twenty minutes to read and can be processed faster than speech, searched, re-read, and shared as a quotable excerpt. The same argument delivered as a podcast requires eighty minutes, cannot be skimmed, and circulates as a recommendation to an already-overwhelmed queue.

item.title: Podcast fatigue is a format problem
item.description: The complaint is always about volume — too many podcasts, not enough time. But the deeper problem is that the format has been applied to content it does not suit.

item.title: Influența otomană în Principatele Române
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Această lucrare examinează mecanismele prin care Principatele Valahia și Moldova au menținut o autonomie administrativă substanțială în cadrul suzeranității otomane, comparativ cu provinciile direct anexate Imperiului.

item.title: New Series: Cities, Data, and the Public Interest
item.description: A new editorial series exploring the intersection of urban infrastructure, digital governance, and civic imagination. The first two essays are live; three more are in preparation.

item.title: The Civic Cost of Platform Monopolies
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: When two or three corporations control the default pathways through which most citizens encounter information, the question is no longer merely economic. It is political, epistemic, and ultimately constitutional.

item.title: Berlin street furniture, an inventory
item.description: Berlin is a city that has rebuilt itself several times within living memory, and it keeps the evidence. The street furniture layers the decades without editing. GDR-era bus shelters stand next to 2010s cycling infrastructure next to Wilhelmine-era lamp standards that somehow survived everything. Nobody rationalised it. The result is illegible as a design system and entirely readable as a history. I spent an afternoon in Prenzlauer Berg photographing things at shin height — utility boxes, kerb edges, drain covers — which is the level at which most of the layering is visible.

item.title: Editorial Board Formed — April 2026
item.description: Academis Demo has established a four-person editorial board to provide oversight of commissioning decisions and to handle appeals from contributors.

item.title: The paywall problem is a funding problem
item.description: Paywalls are not a content strategy. They are a funding strategy that has content consequences. The criticism of paywalls — that they restrict access to information — is correct but incomplete. The more significant effect is what they do to the type of content that gets produced. When revenue depends on subscriptions, you produce content that generates subscription value: exclusivity, depth, a reader identity, a reason to pay. This is not the same set of incentives as producing content with the highest public value.

item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Footnotes in academic history serve two almost incompatible functions: they are simultaneously an audit trail (here is the evidence for this claim) and a parallel argument (here is the context the main text didn’t have space for). When a footnote is doing the second job, it often means the main argument has a load-bearing gap. The solution is usually to move the footnote into the text, not to make it longer.

item.title: Cities at 6am
item.description: The city before it has decided what it is today.

item.title: The Unfinished Argument for Urban Density
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: The empirical case for urban density is strong and largely settled. It reduces carbon emissions, shortens commutes, lowers infrastructure costs, and correlates with better health outcomes. And yet density-positive policy consistently fails at the ballot box. The problem is not the evidence. It is the argument.

item.title: CC BY 4.0 Licence Adopted for All Content
item.description: All Academis Demo publications are now released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International. Existing articles have been retroactively relicensed.

item.title: Normal People — Sally Rooney
item.description: The novel is not about the romance. It is about how two people who are capable of unusual honesty with each other nonetheless consistently choose the wrong silence at the wrong moment.

item.title: Paper Reading Workshop — Spring 2026
item.description: A six-session workshop on close reading of academic papers, held online every other Tuesday. Open to contributors and readers.

item.title: Program de dezbateri academice
item.dc:creator: demo-autor
item.description: Proiectarea și implementarea unui curriculum de dezbateri academice pentru elevi de liceu, cu focus pe argumentare structurată, gândire critică și comunicare publică.

item.title: The primary source problem
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Historians have a professional suspicion of summaries that other disciplines have not yet developed at scale. It comes from experience: we have spent enough time in archives to know that the document described in a secondary source is often not the document in the box. The summary flattens. It removes the hedging, the qualifications, the ambiguous phrasing that the original author included for good reason. It removes the context — who commissioned the document, who would read it, what they were not permitted to say.

item.title: Symposium Report: Evidence in Public Discourse — February 2026
item.description: A brief account of the one-day symposium held in February 2026 on the use and misuse of evidence in journalism, policy, and academic publishing.

item.title: The Science-to-Headline Pipeline and Where It Breaks
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: By the time a scientific finding appears in a general-audience headline, it has passed through a series of transformations — each individually explainable, collectively damaging — that typically leave the original result barely recognisable. This is not a problem of bad actors. It is a problem of misaligned incentives at every stage of the process.

item.title: Corrections Policy Published
item.description: We have published a formal corrections policy covering factual errors, methodological disputes, and retraction criteria.

item.title: Against summaries as a reading practice
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: The summary is a useful tool. Used to revisit what you have already read, or to decide whether something is worth reading, it is entirely legitimate. Used as a substitute for reading — as the primary way you encounter a book, a study, or an argument — it is an epistemic trap. Here is why. An argument is not just a set of conclusions. It is a structure: the order in which premises are introduced, the choice of examples, the qualifications placed on claims, the counter-arguments acknowledged.

item.title: Against summaries as a reading practice
item.description: The summary is a useful tool. Used to revisit what you have already read, or to decide whether something is worth reading, it is entirely legitimate. Used as a substitute for reading — as the primary way you encounter a book, a study, or an argument — it is an epistemic trap. Here is why. An argument is not just a set of conclusions. It is a structure: the order in which premises are introduced, the choice of examples, the qualifications placed on claims, the counter-arguments acknowledged.

item.title: RSS Feed Now Available
item.description: Academis Demo now publishes a full-content RSS feed. Subscribe to receive every new article and announcement in your reader of choice.

item.title: Why Evidence Hierarchies Break Down in Policy Debates
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: The hierarchy of evidence — RCT at the top, expert opinion at the bottom — is a useful heuristic within clinical medicine. Applied uncritically to policy, it produces both overconfidence in what we know and systematic blindness to what we cannot measure.

item.title: Reinhart Koselleck on historical time
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Koselleck’s distinction between Erfahrungsraum (space of experience) and Erwartungshorizont (horizon of expectation) remains the most useful framework I know for thinking about how historical actors understood their own time — not as a fixed point but as a ratio between what has accumulated and what is anticipated. The piece linked here traces how this framework entered anglophone historiography and where it has been applied productively and where it has been stretched past its original meaning.

item.title: What Translation Loses and What That Means for History
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Every historian who works with sources in languages they cannot fully read is doing epistemology under conditions of incomplete information. This is not a methodological failure — it is a condition of the discipline. But it is more consequential than most historical writing acknowledges.

item.title: The Peripheral — William Gibson
item.description: The novel Gibson has been writing since Pattern Recognition finally arrived. It is, among other things, the most technically coherent treatment of time travel I have read.

item.title: Print and the Disruption of Epistemic Authority in Sixteenth-Century Europe
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: This article examines how the rapid proliferation of print between 1517 and 1618 destabilised the mechanisms through which epistemic authority was assigned, contested, and reconstructed in the Holy Roman Empire, France, and England. The argument is that the printing press was not merely a neutral dissemination technology but a structural change in the information market.

item.title: The Summer Book — Tove Jansson
item.description: A novel about being very old and very young at the same time, on a small island, in a summer that is not quite finite.

item.title: The tyranny of the majority
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: “I know of no country in which there is so little independence of mind and real freedom of discussion as in America.” — Tocqueville, Democracy in America, 1835. Still the most uncomfortable observation in the democratic theory canon, because it identifies a failure mode that is entirely consistent with the formal machinery of democracy working correctly. The point is not that majorities make bad decisions. It is that when social conformity is strong enough, the pressure toward majority opinion operates before deliberation rather than after it.

item.title: Tocqueville on the tyranny of the majority
item.description: Still the most uncomfortable observation in the democratic theory canon, because it identifies a failure mode that is entirely consistent with the formal machinery of democracy working correctly. The point is not that majorities make bad decisions. It is that when social conformity is strong enough, the pressure toward majority opinion operates before deliberation rather than after it. People do not reason their way to the consensus view; they sense the consensus and avoid the cost of departing from it.

item.title: Building an Editorial Style Guide for Independent Publications
item.dc:creator: mara-quinn
item.description: A style guide is not a list of spelling preferences. It is a record of editorial decisions — what the publication believes about language, evidence, argument, and its obligations to readers. Most independent publications either have no guide at all or mistake a usage glossary for one.

item.title: Why I write publicly
item.dc:creator: elena-marsh
item.description: Writing publicly is a discipline, not a platform strategy. When I write for myself, I can leave the argument fuzzy at the critical joints. I know what I mean. The reader — which is to say, the future reader, the one who will tell me what I actually wrote — is not there to notice. The moment I write for someone else, the joints have to hold. “This is roughly what I think” becomes an embarrassment.

item.title: Why I write publicly
item.description: Writing publicly is a discipline, not a platform strategy. It forces precision in ways that private thinking does not.

item.title: Transparency Requirements for Automated Decision Systems in Public Administration
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: As public authorities increasingly delegate consequential decisions to automated systems — benefits eligibility, risk scoring, planning approvals — the question of accountability becomes structurally different from the accountability questions we know how to answer. This article maps current regulatory responses and identifies three persistent gaps.

item.title: Inaugural Issue — Call for Submissions
item.description: The first open call for contributions to Academis Demo. We received 34 pitches and commissioned 8 pieces. Submissions are now closed.

item.title: Civic Memory Archive
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: An eighteen-month project to systematically document, structure, and publish the physical evidence of four decades of informal urban adaptation in a former industrial district — before demolition makes the record impossible.

item.title: Open Urban Documentation Initiative
item.dc:creator: thomas-vance
item.description: A two-year project to document, archive, and publish the built fabric of informal housing settlements in three European post-industrial cities — creating a resource that is free, searchable, and maintained by the communities it documents.
